item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably my favorite SCP. Really creative and well done! Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was this time of year two years ago when the youtube algorithm randomly pushed me into learning about SCP, this was one of the articles that definitely helped push past my initial impression of "Oh, so it's some /x-y, murder-monster fan-fic site". Two years and, I dunno, like hundred articles read and it's still one of my favorites. Coming back to re-read it to get in the winter solstice spirit.

That said, the one thing that bothers me about this is the frequency of the ritual. The castration? Sure, big deal. The baby-eating? Yeah, super disturbing, kinda forces you to head-canon clone use for it as a fresh supply of infants seems even less likely than infinite D-class resources. But both those things become comically unrealistic when looking at that time frame. Personally, I would've tweaked it so it only needed to happen once a year during the winter solstice/Saturnalia season. Even the Yule-Man takes the summers off.

But that's just me. I don't expect any changes, that would be ridiculous, it's just my personal opinion. Also, I really want to know what the "hamfisted" ending that everyone was complaining about in the beginning was…. I feel like I should know how to look at previous edits but I still can't figure it out and at this point I'm too embarrassed to ask

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ZK-class big bad EOTW scenario =/= XK-class medium bad EOTW scenario

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear god, it's a deer god.

+1. Man I upvote a lot of stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I admit my upvote is almost exclusively from my almost ridiculous bliss on how many references to greek mythology, anceint religions rites and the like I could spot, and tbh as a recent user, it's an interesting change to apparently be one of the ones that understood the identity the THE DEER almost right away, instead of often feeling out of the loop even after reading the comments :P

I think I read someone asking about why Cronus/Saturn would specifically be a deer, but since the comment was old and I think we aren't suppose to answer to those I'll write my answer here, hope that's okay:

While I don't have much evidence to support this, and I'm likely drawing straws here, I think that it may have to do that in a certain region (in today modern Elis if I remember correctly) of Ancient Greece, the cult to Cronus was celebrated during the month of the spring equinox, in their local calendar (different regions had their own calendars and names for months) this particular month was called Elaphios, which can be translated as "of the deer" basically Cronus was celebrated in the month of the deer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really did enjoy the bizarre and complex containment procedures. At first I thought that the author's comment about ignoring feedback on specific topics was immature, but I kinda of get the point of it now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This remains one of my favorite Series 3 articles of all time. Thematically it's tight and well-detailed. The consultant's note fleshes out the character of THE DEER better than most articles with interview logs, and in the containment procedures to boot. The visuals are striking and the ritual is gruesome without being grotesque and overbearing. I come back to this article a lot just to marvel at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: are pregnant women cloned in SCP-222 the source of all the babies needed for containment here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused by all the negativity this SCP gets (Djoric's posts in this thread from 5 years ago notwithstanding). I found myself enjoying this when I first read it and continue to enjoy it even now.

The containment procedures are so ridiculous because that's the point: this is what it takes to contain a malevolent deity. Here, the ends do justify the means, as horrifying as that is for the Foundation and everyone involved. For all the pain and suffering this thing could cause if it broke free — alongside destroying the Veil — it's worth committing a fraction of that sin to prevent it. Even if it means eating babies.

Now, of course, I don't think the author actually believes in that message. They're illustrating a point about whether the Foundation should be a necessary evil. They supposedly save the world from unfathomably unfair situations all the time by committing comparatively "smaller" acts of injustice. But they put themselves in that position.

Maybe, in enforcing normalcy, they're doing exactly what it takes to prevent others from solving the problem of THE DEER in a far better, more humane way. Without containment.

Djoric was all about destroying "the box". THE DEER stands out to me because it deconstructs the box and makes the reader question whether we'd be better off without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just because you choose to ignore any and all criticism doesn't make your article good. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm never a fan of over-the-top containment procedures. Roasting and eating hundreds of infants every year is so over-the-top and ridiculous it's almost laughable. Plus, I will never not view infanticide as a cheap trope that adds nothing but easy shock value.

The linked addendum at the very end tells me nothing, and so did the article itself until I did some further reading about its inspirations. Which imho I shouldn't really have to do in order to understand an article, but whatever maybe that's just me.

I commend the author for the design and description of the deer itself. Very imposing, even if I somewhat felt like I'd seen it before in form of Arceus from Pokémon. Anyhow I quite liked the SCP itself, just not the article constructed around it sooo downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It only works because the Deer decided that it works. Something something Saturnalia. It could break out if it thought it could, but it doesn't, because it's been fooled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't really sure quite how to feel about this one when I read it and came to the comment section to think it over. Reading some of the reactions to this, somehow, has solidified this as a downvote for me, and it's entirely for the containment procedures. I like the addendum about rituals and what they mean, but the thing is… it still just feels so… arbitrary and over the top? Yeah, okay, rituals are meaningful for the sake of rituals and all, but these are rituals that someone devised in living memory, and their entire explanation for it basically amounts to "Just trust me on this." Maybe I'm just missing it, but I don't see any hint as to why the containment procedures are what they are, and without that it feels sort of like the author stepping in to the narrative to say "suspend your disbelief and trust me on this." Without at least a sliver of insight as to how the rituals relate to the containment, it feels like anything could be substituted in there, and that loses the power of it. If there are "powerful symbols" in there, then I'd like a sense of what they are and why they work, not a list of rehashed ritual tropes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does Saturn have to do with turning people into pillars of brain matter? You went into a lot of detail with that, and I see literally no significance of it in relation to Saturn… or THE DEER for that matter.

E: Also, why a DEER specifically? I may be missing something here, but deer aren't really what comes to mind when I think of Saturn. Time manipulation maybe (which THE DEER doesn't have) but deer?

EE: Apparently, this is some sort of trickster god, referenced in other Skips.
…I'm just not seeing it? The Trickster is always depicted as slightly weaker than the other gods, and made it to the top through pranks and wits. He doesn't command any force of nature, and usually controls something that human beings came up with. On a technicality, he may not be a god at all.
THIS GUY is stoic and mighty, solemn, and The Trickster would probably see him as boring. He controls the air itself, and exerts its mighty force like the best of them. It'd be sitting up at the top of the Great Pantheon, at the right hand of the Supreme Leader.
I didn't even downvote until I realized this! This is a needless crosslink that doesn't really make sense in my mind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description of the skip actually reminded me of the avatars in Endless Forest, though obviously much larger and with a long neck. The imagery is fantastic, actually, and I'm still thinking about 2845's appearance as I type this; I might try to draw it later. The concept of 2845-1 is very interesting to me and I really, really wish there was more about those.

On the other hand, the baby-eating earned a quick -1. The only way I connected this article to Cronus - or to Saturn at all, really - is through reading the comments, though I suppose that's my bad for reading this while sick and not remembering in-jokes until after the fact. Regardless, it reads as shock value and humans obviously aren't this thing's children anyway.

The consultant's note was jarring - I actually somehow thought I started reading a note from the author and was baffled until I got to the second paragraph. The tale attached to the end didn't really do anything for me either, but maybe I just don't Get It.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just dont find it interesting. The containment procedures read like an aristocrats joke, overblown and verging on rediculous with baby eating and castration, just feels hamfisted.
-1 sorry, this just doesnt work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand all the hate for this skip. I really enjoyed the excessive rituals for being just that, rituals. I dunno, must be biased for loving a cervid god being with metallic gasses.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seen this one a few times before, given it's in the same series as my own, but I never read back to see the comments of others.

I honestly never picked up on the masquerade being broken, but I still think this article is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never thought I'd be glad an article exists. I am that the Saturn Deer does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I logged back in after fifty million years just to say a bit about breaking the masquerade:

A lot of the comments have pointed out that the Foundation has successfully contained bigger or more significant events before. While true, I don't think that's relevant, for the reason that past performance is no guarantee of future performance.

The central beast itself is a good solid freaky-ass Foundation anomaly. Its containment procedures like an amalgamation of a lot of high-complexity entries. Other than the breaking of the masquerade, this is a pretty good example of a powerful, dangerous, well-written skip. It may not be "the most X" or "the biggest Y" or whatever. There ARE big bads and horrible things that exceed it in various metrics.

But it doesn't need to be the most dangerous/powerful/spooky-doopy anything in order to break the masquerade. The masquerade is broken when the Foundation doesn't succeed. On this one, in that one particular way, the Foundation missed a detail, or hit the killswitch a second too late, or didn't get the right amnestics to the right people, or had their MTF team just half a kilometer off the mark, or whatever. Somewhere along the line, a ball was dropped, and the masquerade broke.

It could have happened with any skip. It could happen with Keter cakes. It could happen with goddamn Cassie.

It happened with the Deer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself weirdly possessive of this article. I can't help but get annoyed whenever I see "it's too gratuitous" because I feel like those people are missing the point, and I can't help but get frustrated when I see "but it breaks the masquerade" because who gives two shits.

At the same time, anyone is more than welcome to think whatever they want about about anything, and who am I to say otherwise.

That being said, this is probably in my top 5 articles on the site. It does what it does better than anything I've ever seen try to do the same, both here and elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The baby-eating is a dealbreaker for me, too. I get that it's meant to echo the Saturn myth, but based on what that consultant wrote, there's no reason why it needs to echo the Saturn myth. Yes, from what he's saying it makes sense that the ritual can't be changed now, but if rituals don't follow laws and are basically arbitrary, then there's no reason why they had to include baby-eating (or castration, for that matter) to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1 for gratuitous baby-eating.

Not words I thought I'd have to use for such an otherwise well-written article but… gratuitous baby-eating isn't something the Foundation would condone. There is absolutely no reason for this thing to require baby-eating, the article even says so:

Rituals do not work because of some underlying laws, such as those that science operates on. Rituals work because they are rituals. They work because an arbitrary set of criteria has been met with exacting care. Belief that meeting these arbitrary criteria achieves a certain end assigns power to the ritual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last year, I took an Anthropology course, and right after hearing a lecture about how rituals define and give meaning to our realities I read an excerpt from this skip ("Rituals do not work because of some underlying laws…" to be specific) after explaining the basic context to him. Even with the context, it's almost a perfect description of how anthropologists see ritual in society.

item.title: Ridiculous
item.content:encoded:
This is ridiculous. Just ridiculous and over the top. The procedures themselves look like something you could read in a J article. I'm sorry, but I don't understand the likes.

-1

item.title: Explain to a n00b
item.content:encoded: …who understands the Saturn mythos and applauds your use. How do the rituals, exactly, distract the god and keep it sated? Our Judeo-God once took pleasure in the fumes from Abel's sacrifice, the Greeks were similarly pleased by burning flesh, Christ is invoked through trans and consubstantiation in Catholicism: how does this God beyond thought take its pleasure from the rituals, whatever they happen to be? If I'm missing something, do let me know. If this is a God on the order of the Godhead in the Sefer Yetzirah, a time and place that is neither time nor place—even there the angels singing around Keter are pleasing and define the Godhead. So what is the relationship here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a neat idea, smart use of both myth and science, cool monster, and an entertainingly freaky disaster scenario. The unusual format and broken masquerade thing don't put me off at all, because such things should always be available options to a writer letting their creative stuff flow. The only concern is whether it's used competently, which in my opinion here it most certainly is.

Nonetheless, by the time I got here this SCP had been overhyped just a bit…albeit, part of that might have to do with my otherwise excellent choice to subscribe to SunnyClockwork's DeviantArt page. Seeing Djoric having to explain the baby-eating and other procedural horrors not just every other page of the comment thread but in the "containment consultant" note, wherein the voice of the author is loud, turns into bad comedy.

To compare: SCP-089, in my opinion, is one of the most genuinely unsettling, even upsetting articles on the site. Which is to say, it's very, very good, in that horrific Requiem-for-a-Dream-I'm-never-looking-at-this-again way. But, the way that element is used in this one, it elicits nothing more than a massive eyeroll. We see what the idea is, for sure, but nevertheless that's my takeaway from it.

My vote's neutral. The Deer THE DEER can hang out, even if it's a huge dork.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, this. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Logged in for the express purpose of upvoting this. I liked it a lot. The Deer (THE DEER?) puts me in the mind of one of Exalted's Yozis.

It's late and I might not be reading too closely, but it seems like the ending is a little… abrupt? I feel like I'm lacking a sense of panic leading into the masquerade breaking, but maybe that's intentional.

Also threat levels feel like something that should be around anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet another uninteresting reality-ender that lacks any subtlety. Oh, look. Abortions and baby eating on the Secure Containment Procedures.

I actually quite like this skip for what it is, but if you don't, you could always consider this to be a case of SCP-2130-J infection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another uninteresting reality-ender that lacks any subtlety. Oh, look. Abortions and baby eating on the Secure Containment Procedures. Over-the-top abilities, it's hard to kill so we need our illuminati bush 05 to read it bedtime stories. It kills people really fast and level four access is required. Surprising, that's never been done before!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot that I like about this, but I found the overall execution, in my mind, to be a little over-the-top. I thought that the foundation official's message refusing to simplify the containment procedures was quite dramatic, and portions of it seem to elicit the feeling that there's inside jokes in here that I don't understand (such as the naming of the article "THE DEER"). I know that the author is very highly regarded, and that in of itself gives me the impression that I may simply just not have "gotten" the concept. However, it is what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, you could do it by reflecting white light off it to get an approximation of its absorption spectrum. You'd be able to tell easily if it were pure hydrogen or helium.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or is the Saturn Magic Square (a 3x3 magic square where everything adds up to 15) missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just for the record, the planetary body was not "arbitrarily named" for the Greek myth. The Greeks believed that the planetary bodies they saw in the sky WERE the gods they worshipped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was a huge buildup to a joke termination procedure. A researcher just walks up to the SCP, says "$299", and it drops dead.

As is all the saturn puns don't really have any payoff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't enjoy this SCP. I'll go through the text in order from start to finish.

The containment procedures were long and I didn't find them interesting. I just found them tedious to read through. The message from the containment basically said in a long-winded way that we are incapable of comprehending how this thing thinks. Okay, that's a concept that's been done before and it doesn't give anything to think about, so I didn't find that interesting. It's also stated that the SCP is basically omnipotent and will destroy everything if let out of its containment, so there's no interacting with it of any kind.

So, this SCP didn't scare me or interest me. I just felt bored and a bit confused reading it. I read in a previous comment that a lot of the things in the SCP were references to the planet/god Saturn. If I had gotten those referenced, they may have been fun and interesting to me. But I didn't, so they weren't.

For the reasons above, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps it's all a question of definition. I don't ascribe to a strict Dawkinsian definition of memes (in much the same way strict Mendelian and Lamarkcian definitions of genes have been found to be incomplete).

If there's anything that makes me wonder, though, is whether "memetic hazards" are actually "cognitohazards."

But here we're moving away from THE DEER. We can probably better discuss this in a different thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the purpose of this question to maintain a belief that knowledge carries a higher order of reality than action?

The purpose of the question is to ask if a behavior is a meme or if it is the result of the expression of ideas (composed according to memetics of memes). If behavior is memetic expression then isn't ritual more protein synthesis?

I honestly don't know, I find the memetic analogy to be pretty confusing. Fortunately it currently seems to be a less than adequate model for cultural replication.

I'm not sure why genes are being invoked, here.

Aren't genes invoked by analogy every time you invoke memes? Memes are the discrete replicators of culture the way that genes are the discrete replicators of biology, at least according to my copy of The Selfish Gene.

I don't really think memes are analogous to genes.

How is that even possible? Dawkins nearly exactly defined them as precisely that: a replicator for culture that works in analogy to genes.

The pathways of expression and multiplication are different

I agree that appears to be the case, but that just means the memetic model isn't accurate. However, that is real life, in the scipverse memetics is apparently valid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is correct. THE DEER would be immune to memetic threats because it does not think. It is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But memes do not work because the subject believes in their power. They work because they affect the brain. I think the word "memetics" does not apply in this case. No brain = no mems. Or maybe my understanding of memes is too narrow?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the purpose of this question to maintain a belief that knowledge carries a higher order of reality than action? I'm not sure why genes are being invoked, here.

ETA: I suppose that's one way to look at it, but I don't really think memes are analogous to genes. The pathways of expression and multiplication are different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not in the usual sense, no. A memetic kill switch wouldn't work unless The Deer believed it worked.

But what is the ritual, if not a memetic containment switch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If memes are analogous to genes, then wouldn't behaviors (like rituals) be analogous to proteins?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I understand right, memetics is based on some brain stimulation. It's some combination of stimuli like flashes of light which lead to epileptic seizure (the simple example from real life). The question is does the Deer have a brain or a mentality in the usual sense?

Upd: After reading your ETA I think that it makes a lot of sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it works, is it deception?

ETA: At its core, rituals are memes. They are specified actions which are undertaken because they carry a widespread belief among its participants. The actions support the belief. Rituals have expressed and codified cultures for humanity for millennia before the advent of writing, and they continue to do so to this day. Even today, even if you're an atheist, rituals define your belief system. You go to the corner store, you ask for a burger, someone makes one and hands it to you, you pull out some paper and metal discs, you give them to the person, and you take your burger and no one complains. This all works because we all believe it works.

In the case of a god, memetic reality is physical reality. The physical truth is what the god believes to be true - it thinks it, it happens. Thus, when the god believes a ritual will contain it, then the ritual actually contains it. A god cannot be deceived, ultimately, because it cannot believe something that isn't actually happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose rituals and memes are not the same. Rituals are deceiving the Deer, making it think that it's contained, am I right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why the containment works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Deer is resistant to physical damage. But what about memes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Close enough. The way I imagine it has a space between each ring, but that's not super important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Site 100 description is not very clear, our translators can't come to a single opinion about what it looks like.
Is this design correct?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stuff like this is why people started not worshipping their gods.

Eating a baby twice a week is over-the-top, ESPECIALLY because you could have just had them eat the stones mentioned in the containment and you didn't.

Downvote for insane amounts of baby-eating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Update: I still love everything about this and it should be much higher rated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ammonia has no vapor pressure at -110C and 2.3 bar, and cannot form any portion of an atmosphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That still doesn't explain how they know what the spheres are, unless they are making assumptions about their composition because a non-human entity that has either never been to Earth or has not been here in a very, very long time somehow makes the same association between a human myth and a different planetary body arbitrarily named for said myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, but it still doesn't explain how they know what it is in the first place, since they can't exactly walk up and test it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Castration and baby-eating are included in the containment procedures entirely for shock-value

Consider…

Whose myth has castration and baby-eating? Saturn.

When was the fragment confirmed to hit Earth? Saturnalia.

What's up with that one seemingly random play in the containment procedures? Also Saturnalia.

What does the planet Saturn have? A ring of ice particles.

How many primary moons does Saturn have? Seven: Mimas, Enceladaus, Tethys, Dione, Rhea, Titan and Iapetus.

What is the atmospheric makeup of Saturn? Hydrogen, helium and ammonia.

What's underneath that atmosphere? Liquid metallic hydrogen and liquid metallic helium.

In a fight between humans and an elder god, who wins? (Spoilers: it's the elder god)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Helium is not a metal, and no amount of reality-bending would make it so without causing it to no longer be identifiable as helium.

Actually, the pressure and temperature inside gas giants can turn helium and hydrogen into liquid metal. It doesn't even take reality bending; reality is just really weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As non-standard as it is, good on you for making a highly rated article with a sub class/treat level.
